lesson: A beater that hums, stalls and trips with healthy windings but a dead run capacitor is the textbook test-before-you-swap fault. The motor is fine; condemning it is the expensive miss.
symptoms: Trips out after a few seconds.

tests.error-log: BEATER OVERCURRENT trip on every start attempt since 6 am.
tests.inspect-beater: Beater spins freely by hand. No ice, no seizure, nothing jammed.
tests.continuity-test: Motor windings read perfect. The run capacitor reads dead — it's gone.

symptoms: Loud hum then a click from behind the panel; the barrel never turns.
Worked fine at close last night.

tests.error-log: BEATER OVERCURRENT on every start attempt since opening.
tests.inspect-beater: Beater turns freely by hand — nothing jammed, no ice.
tests.continuity-test: Windings in spec. Run capacitor measures a fraction of its rated microfarads — it's gone.

symptoms: Beater only starts if someone spins the drive by hand first.
Dies again on the next cycle.

tests.inspect-beater: Free to rotate by hand. No mechanical resistance at all.
tests.continuity-test: Motor windings healthy. The run capacitor is bulged at the top and reads dead.

flavour: Saved a nine-hundred-dollar motor with a twenty-dollar can of electrons.
authenticityNote: A dead run capacitor makes a perfectly healthy single-phase motor hum, stall and trip — the textbook test-before-you-swap fault (GDD §2.2). Condemning the motor is the classic expensive wrong answer.
